pathophysiology_summary: Plantar fasciitis is a degenerative/overload injury of the plantar fascia at its medial calcaneal origin, provoked by unsupportive footwear, hard surfaces, increased load and calf tightness. The hallmark is sharp plantar heel pain worst on the first steps after rest (the fascia contracts and is re-stretched on weight-bearing), easing with initial activity and worsening again after prolonged standing, with point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tubercle and a positive windlass test. It is benign but often protracted (months). The ground-truth task is to recognise this pattern and distinguish it from a calcaneal stress fracture (night/rest pain, positive calcaneal squeeze), Achilles rupture (sudden pop, loss of push-off, positive Thompson), and tarsal tunnel syndrome (burning/tingling neuropathic pain); imaging is NOT indicated in the acute phase, and management is stretching, footwear/orthotic support, activity modification and analgesia.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic first-step plantar heel pain after unsupportive footwear on hard floors.
Screen for night/rest pain and calcaneal-squeeze pain.
Sudden pop / loss of push-off would suggest rupture; not present.
Neuropathic burning/tingling would suggest this; the pain here is mechanical.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Plantar fasciitis leads; stress fracture, Achilles rupture and tarsal tunnel should be screened before conservative management.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic pattern, no night/rest pain or calcaneal-squeeze pain, no pop/push-off loss, no neuropathic features — plantar fasciitis; no imaging needed.
No night/rest pain or squeeze pain; escalation target if they develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative red-flag screen, plantar fasciitis leads; conservative management with a stress-fracture safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; often takes months; physiotherapy/podiatry if persistent.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign plantar fasciitis.

red_flags.description: Calcaneal stress fracture features — heel pain at night/rest, diffuse pain on squeezing the sides of the heel bone, or a change to a constant dull throbbing ache
red_flags.clinical_significance: A stress fracture needs imaging and offloading, not fascia stretching.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: X-ray is not useful acutely for plantar fasciitis (a soft-tissue condition).
Not screening night/rest pain or calcaneal squeeze in heel pain.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Precise palpation of the calcaneal tubercle / windlass and Thompson tests by the clinician
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Stretching + supportive footwear/orthotics + activity modification + analgesia; podiatry/physio if persistent; review/imaging if stress-fracture features develop.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Plantar fasciitis is clinical; imaging is not required initially. Manage with plantar-fascia and calf stretching, supportive footwear/orthoses, activity/load modification and simple analgesia; refer to podiatry/physiotherapy if persistent.
